    38вҶ’  `вҲҖ i, f i = g i`. Use `exact h i`."
    39вҶ’  exact h i
    40вҶ’
    41вҶ’Conclusion "
    42вҶ’The pattern is simple:
    43вҶ’
    44вҶ’1. `ext i` вҖ” reduce function equality to pointwise equality
    45вҶ’2. Prove `f i = g i` for the introduced `i`
    46вҶ’
    47вҶ’This is the same tactic you used in MeetFin for `Fin` elements.
    48вҶ’Both are instances of the same principle: two composite objects
    49вҶ’are equal when their components are equal.
    50вҶ’
    51вҶ’Note: the tactic `funext i` does the same thing as `ext i`
    52вҶ’for functions. Both are valid.
    53вҶ’"
    54вҶ’
    55вҶ’/-- `ext` reduces equality of composite objects to equality of their components.
    56вҶ’
    57вҶ’## For Fin elements
    58вҶ’When the goal is `a = b` where `a b : Fin n`, `ext` reduces it to
    59вҶ’`a.val = b.val` вҖ” two `Fin` values are equal when their underlying
    60вҶ’natural numbers are equal.
    61вҶ’
    62вҶ’## For functions
    63вҶ’When the goal is `f = g` where `f g : Оұ вҶ’ ОІ`, `ext i` introduces
    64вҶ’a variable `i : Оұ` and reduces the goal to `f i = g i` вҖ” two
    65вҶ’functions are equal when they agree on every input.
    66вҶ’
    67вҶ’## For finsets
    68вҶ’When the goal is `s = t` where `s t : Finset Оұ`, `ext x` introduces
    69вҶ’a variable `x : Оұ` and reduces the goal to `x вҲҲ s вҶ” x вҲҲ t` вҖ” two
    70вҶ’finsets are equal when they have the same elements (extensionality).
    71вҶ’
    72вҶ’All are instances of the same principle: two composite objects are

import Game.Metadata

World "FinTuples"
Level 12

Title "ext for Functions"

Introduction "
# Extensionality for Functions

In MeetFin, you used `ext` to prove two `Fin` elements are
equal by showing their underlying values are equal. Now you'll
use `ext` in a new context: **functions**.

For functions, extensionality says:

> Two functions are equal if and only if they agree on every input.

In symbols: $f = g \\iff \\forall\\, i,\\; f(i) = g(i)$.

When the goal is `f = g` where `f g : Fin n вҶ’ Оұ`, using
`ext i` introduces `i : Fin n` and changes the goal to `f i = g i`.

This is the same `ext` tactic, just applied to a different type.
In MeetFin, `ext` decomposed `Fin` equality into value equality.
Here, `ext` decomposes function equality into pointwise equality.

**Your task**: Prove `f = g` given that they agree on every input.
"

/-- Two functions that agree everywhere are equal. -/
Statement (f g : Fin 3 вҶ’ в„•) (h : вҲҖ i, f i = g i) :
    f = g := by
  Hint "Use `ext i` to introduce an index `i : Fin 3` and
  reduce the goal to `f i = g i`."
  ext i
  Hint "The goal is `f i = g i`. The hypothesis `{h}` says
  `вҲҖ i, f i = g i`. Use `exact h i`."
  exact h i

Conclusion "
The pattern is simple:

1. `ext i` вҖ” reduce function equality to pointwise equality
2. Prove `f i = g i` for the introduced `i`

This is the same tactic you used in MeetFin for `Fin` elements.
Both are instances of the same principle: two composite objects
are equal when their components are equal.

Note: the tactic `funext i` does the same thing as `ext i`
for functions. Both are valid.
"

/-- `ext` reduces equality of composite objects to equality of their components.

## For Fin elements
When the goal is `a = b` where `a b : Fin n`, `ext` reduces it to
`a.val = b.val` вҖ” two `Fin` values are equal when their underlying
natural numbers are equal.

## For functions
When the goal is `f = g` where `f g : Оұ вҶ’ ОІ`, `ext i` introduces
a variable `i : Оұ` and reduces the goal to `f i = g i` вҖ” two
functions are equal when they agree on every input.

## For finsets
When the goal is `s = t` where `s t : Finset Оұ`, `ext x` introduces
a variable `x : Оұ` and reduces the goal to `x вҲҲ s вҶ” x вҲҲ t` вҖ” two
finsets are equal when they have the same elements (extensionality).

All are instances of the same principle: two composite objects are
equal when their components are equal.
-/
TacticDoc ext

DisabledTheorem funext

1вҶ’import Game.Metadata
     2вҶ’
     3вҶ’World "FinTuples"
     4вҶ’Level 21
     5вҶ’
     6вҶ’Title "Boss: Two Strategies, Two Tuples"
     7вҶ’
     8вҶ’Introduction "
     9вҶ’# Boss: Proving Tuples Are Equal
    10вҶ’
    11вҶ’This boss has two parts, testing both reconstruction strategies.
    12вҶ’
    13вҶ’**Part 1 вҖ” Front reconstruction**: You're given tuples `f` and `g`
    14вҶ’described in different ways:
    15вҶ’- `f` by its head and tail: `hf0 : f 0 = 1`, `hft : Fin.tail f = ![2, 3]`
    16вҶ’- `g` pointwise: `hg : forall i, g i = i.val + 1`
    17вҶ’
    18вҶ’Use the **cons reconstruction** from Level 20 to determine `f`,
    19вҶ’then `ext` + case split to verify `f = g`.
    20вҶ’
    21вҶ’**Part 2 вҖ” Back reconstruction**: You're given a tuple `p`
    22вҶ’described by its init and last element:
    23вҶ’- `hpi : Fin.init p = ![10, 20]`
    24вҶ’- `hpl : p (Fin.last 2) = 30`
    25вҶ’
    26вҶ’Use the **snoc reconstruction** (`vecSnoc_self_init` + `.symm`)
    27вҶ’to determine `p = vecSnoc ![10, 20] 30`.
    28вҶ’
    29вҶ’This boss combines:
    30вҶ’- `Fin.cons_self_tail` + `.symm` + `rw ... at` (front reconstruction)
    31вҶ’- `vecSnoc_self_init` + `.symm` + `rw ... at` (back reconstruction)
    32вҶ’- `ext` + nested `cases` (pointwise verification)
    33вҶ’- `rw` with a universally quantified hypothesis
    34вҶ’"
    35вҶ’
    36вҶ’/-- Two tuples reconstructed from different ends. -/
    37вҶ’Statement (f g : Fin 3 вҶ’ в„•)
    38вҶ’    (hf0 : f 0 = 1) (hft : Fin.tail f = ![2, 3])
    39вҶ’    (hg : вҲҖ i : Fin 3, g i = i.val + 1)
    40вҶ’    (p : Fin 3 вҶ’ в„•)
    41вҶ’    (hpi : Fin.init p = ![10, 20])
    42вҶ’    (hpl : p (Fin.last 2) = 30) :
    43вҶ’    f = g вҲ§ p = vecSnoc ![10, 20] 30 := by
    44вҶ’  Hint "The goal is a conjunction. Use `constructor` to split
    45вҶ’  into Part 1 and Part 2."
    46вҶ’  constructor
    47вҶ’  -- Part 1: front reconstruction + pointwise verification
    48вҶ’  В· Hint "Part 1: determine `f` using cons reconstruction.
    49вҶ’    `have hf : f = ![1, 2, 3] := by`
    50вҶ’    then inside: `have key := (Fin.cons_self_tail f).symm`
    51вҶ’    followed by `rw [hf0, hft] at key` and `exact key`."
    52вҶ’    have hf : f = ![1, 2, 3] := by
    53вҶ’      have key := (Fin.cons_self_tail f).symm
    54вҶ’      rw [hf0, hft] at key
    55вҶ’      exact key
    56вҶ’    Hint "Good! Now use `ext вҹЁv, hvвҹ©` to reduce `f = g` to
    57вҶ’    checking each index."
    58вҶ’    ext вҹЁv, hvвҹ©
    59вҶ’    Hint "Rewrite `f` using `hf` and `g` using `hg`:
    60вҶ’    `rw [hf, hg]`."
    61вҶ’    rw [hf, hg]
    62вҶ’    Hint "Now case-split on `v` to verify each index."
    63вҶ’    Hint (hidden := true) "`cases v with | zero => rfl | succ n => ...`"
    64вҶ’    cases v with
    65вҶ’    | zero =>
    66вҶ’      Hint (hidden := true) "Both sides evaluate to 1. Use `rfl`."
    67вҶ’      rfl
    68вҶ’    | succ n =>
    69вҶ’      Hint (hidden := true) "Continue: `cases n with | zero => rfl | succ m => ...`"
    70вҶ’      cases n with
    71вҶ’      | zero => rfl
    72вҶ’      | succ m =>
    73вҶ’        Hint (hidden := true) "`cases m with | zero => rfl | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)`"
    74вҶ’        cases m with
    75вҶ’        | zero => rfl
    76вҶ’        | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)
    77вҶ’  -- Part 2: back reconstruction
    78вҶ’  В· Hint "Part 2: determine `p` using snoc reconstruction.
    79вҶ’    Use `have key := (vecSnoc_self_init p).symm`."
    80вҶ’    have key := (vecSnoc_self_init p).symm
    81вҶ’    Hint "Now substitute the known last element and init:
    82вҶ’    `rw [hpl, hpi] at key`."
    83вҶ’    rw [hpl, hpi] at key
    84вҶ’    Hint "Now `key` matches the goal. Use `exact key`."
    85вҶ’    exact key
    86вҶ’
    87вҶ’Conclusion "
    88вҶ’Congratulations вҖ” you've completed **Fin Tuples**!
    89вҶ’
    90вҶ’Here's your tuple toolkit:
    91вҶ’
    92вҶ’| Tool | What it does | Learned in |
    93вҶ’|---|---|---|
    94вҶ’| `![a, b, c]` | Build tuple with vec notation | Levels 1-2 |
    95вҶ’| `use` + `constructor` | Construct and verify a tuple | Level 3 |
    96вҶ’| `Fin.cons a f` | Prepend element to tuple | Level 4 |
    97вҶ’| `Fin.cons_val_zero` | `Fin.cons a f 0 = a` | Level 5 |
    98вҶ’| `Fin.cons_val_succ` | `Fin.cons a f i.succ = f i` | Level 5 |
    99вҶ’| `Fin.tail f` | Drop the first element | Level 6 |
   100вҶ’| `Fin.cons_self_tail f` | `cons (f 0) (tail f) = f` | Level 7 |
   101вҶ’| Round-trip | `tail (cons a g) = g` and `(cons a g) 0 = a` | Level 8 |
   102вҶ’| `vecSnoc f x` | Append element to tuple | Level 9 |
   103вҶ’| `vecSnoc_last` / `vecSnoc_castSucc` | Access snoc'd elements | Level 9 |
   104вҶ’| `Fin.init f` | Drop the last element | Level 10 |
   105вҶ’| `vecSnoc_self_init f` | `vecSnoc (init f) (f (last n)) = f` | Level 11 |
   106вҶ’| `ext i` | Reduce function equality to pointwise | Level 12 |
   107вҶ’| Tuple composition | `g circ f` transforms tuples | Level 13 |
   108вҶ’| Tuples as data | Verify concrete computations on tuples | Level 14 |
   109вҶ’| Cons injectivity | Equal cons'd tuples have equal parts | Level 15 |
   110вҶ’| Snoc injectivity | Equal snoc'd tuples have equal parts | Level 16 |
   111вҶ’| `Fin.elim0 i` | Eliminate impossible `Fin 0` | Level 17 |
   112вҶ’| `ext` + case split | Prove `f = ![...]` by index | Level 18 |
   113вҶ’| `.symm` | Flip an equation | Level 19 |
   114вҶ’| Reconstruction | `.symm` + `rw at` from head/tail or init/last | Level 20 |
   115вҶ’
   116вҶ’**Proof strategies**:
   117вҶ’- **Index-by-index** (`ext` + `cases`): when you know each value
   118вҶ’- **Front reconstruction** (`cons_self_tail.symm` + `rw`): when you know head + tail
   119вҶ’- **Back reconstruction** (`vecSnoc_self_init.symm` + `rw`): when you know init + last
   120вҶ’
   121вҶ’**The big picture**: `Fin n -> alpha` models ordered tuples. `Fin.cons` builds
   122вҶ’from the front, `vecSnoc` from the back. Both have decomposition (tail/init)
   123вҶ’and reconstruction (cons_self_tail/vecSnoc_self_init) identities. Function
   124вҶ’extensionality (`ext`) lets you prove two tuples are equal index by index.
   125вҶ’The round-trip identities (cons then tail gives back the same tuple, and
   126вҶ’vice versa) mean that `Fin.cons` and `(head, tail)` form a **bijection**
   127вҶ’between `alpha x (Fin n -> alpha)` and `Fin (n+1) -> alpha`. In later
   128вҶ’worlds, you'll formalize this idea as an `Equiv` (equivalence).
   129вҶ’
   130вҶ’**Where this is going**: Tuples are the building blocks for **big operators**
   131вҶ’like $\\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} f(i)$, written `\\sum i, f i` in Lean. Summing the
   132вҶ’entries of a tuple `f : Fin n \\to \\mathbb{N}` is exactly what big operators
   133вҶ’do вҖ” and that's where this course is headed after Finset.
   134вҶ’
   135вҶ’Next up: the Pset (problem set) world, which will test these skills
   136вҶ’on fresh problems without scaffolding.
   137вҶ’"
   138вҶ’
   139вҶ’DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num
   140вҶ’DisabledTheorem funext
   141вҶ’

"

Conclusion "
Congratulations вҖ” you've completed **Fin Tuples**!

Here's your tuple toolkit:

| Tool | What it does | Learned in |

| `![a, b, c]` | Build tuple with vec notation | Levels 1-2 |

| Round-trip | `tail (cons a g) = g` and `(cons a g) 0 = a` | Level 8 |
| `vecSnoc f x` | Append element to tuple | Level 9 |
| `vecSnoc_last` / `vecSnoc_castSucc` | Access snoc'd elements | Level 9 |
| `Fin.init f` | Drop the last element | Level 10 |
| `vecSnoc_self_init f` | `vecSnoc (init f) (f (last n)) = f` | Level 11 |
| `ext i` | Reduce function equality to pointwise | Level 12 |
| Tuple composition | `g circ f` transforms tuples | Level 13 |
| Tuples as data | Verify concrete computations on tuples | Level 14 |
| Cons injectivity | Equal cons'd tuples have equal parts | Level 15 |
| Snoc injectivity | Equal snoc'd tuples have equal parts | Level 16 |
| `Fin.elim0 i` | Eliminate impossible `Fin 0` | Level 17 |
| `ext` + case split | Prove `f = ![...]` by index | Level 18 |
| `.symm` | Flip an equation | Level 19 |
| Reconstruction | `.symm` + `rw at` from head/tail or init/last | Level 20 |

**Proof strategies**:
- **Index-by-index** (`ext` + `cases`): when you know each value
- **Front reconstruction** (`cons_self_tail.symm` + `rw`): when you know head + tail
- **Back reconstruction** (`vecSnoc_self_init.symm` + `rw`): when you know init + last

**The big picture**: `Fin n -> alpha` models ordered tuples. `Fin.cons` builds
from the front, `vecSnoc` from the back. Both have decomposition (tail/init)
and reconstruction (cons_self_tail/vecSnoc_self_init) identities. Function
extensionality (`ext`) lets you prove two tuples are equal index by index.
The round-trip identities (cons then tail gives back the same tuple, and
vice versa) mean that `Fin.cons` and `(head, tail)` form a **bijection**
between `alpha x (Fin n -> alpha)` and `Fin (n+1) -> alpha`. In later
worlds, you'll formalize this idea as an `Equiv` (equivalence).

**Where this is going**: Tuples are the building blocks for **big operators**
like $\\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} f(i)$, written `\\sum i, f i` in Lean. Summing the
entries of a tuple `f : Fin n \\to \\mathbb{N}` is exactly what big operators
do вҖ” and that's where this course is headed after Finset.

Next up: the Pset (problem set) world, which will test these skills
on fresh problems without scaffolding.
"

DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num
DisabledTheorem funext

     5вҶ’
     6вҶ’Title "The Pigeonhole Principle (Concrete)"
     7вҶ’
     8вҶ’Introduction "
     9вҶ’# Three Pigeons, Two Holes
    10вҶ’
    11вҶ’The **pigeonhole principle** says: if you put more objects into
    12вҶ’fewer boxes, at least two objects share a box.
    13вҶ’
    14вҶ’Formally: any function `f : Fin 3 -> Fin 2` must send two
    15вҶ’different inputs to the same output. There are 3 inputs but
    16вҶ’only 2 possible outputs, so a collision is inevitable.
    17вҶ’
    18вҶ’This is your first real **theorem from combinatorics** вҖ” and
    19вҶ’you can prove it using only the tools from Phase 1.
    20вҶ’
    21вҶ’**Strategy**:
    22вҶ’1. Classify each `Fin 2` value: every `x : Fin 2` is either `0` or `1`
    23вҶ’2. Apply the classification to `f 0` and `f 1`
    24вҶ’3. If they're equal: witnesses are `0` and `1`
    25вҶ’4. If they differ: check `f 2` вҖ” it must match one of them
    26вҶ’"
    27вҶ’
    28вҶ’/-- Any function from Fin 3 to Fin 2 has a collision. -/
    29вҶ’Statement (f : Fin 3 вҶ’ Fin 2) : вҲғ i j : Fin 3, i вү  j вҲ§ f i = f j := by
    30вҶ’  -- Helper: every Fin 2 element is 0 or 1
    31вҶ’  Hint "First, prove a helper: every element of `Fin 2` is `0` or `1`.
    32вҶ’  Use `have classify : forall x : Fin 2, x = 0 or x = 1 := by ...`"
    33вҶ’  Hint (hidden := true) "Inside the `have`, destructure:
    34вҶ’  `intro (v, hv); cases v with | zero => left; rfl | succ n => ...`
    35вҶ’  For `succ n`: `cases n with | zero => right; rfl | succ m => exact absurd hv (by omega)`"
    36вҶ’  have classify : вҲҖ x : Fin 2, x = 0 вҲЁ x = 1 := by
    37вҶ’    intro вҹЁv, hvвҹ©
    38вҶ’    cases v with
    39вҶ’    | zero => left; rfl
    40вҶ’    | succ n =>
    41вҶ’      cases n with
    42вҶ’      | zero => right; rfl
    43вҶ’      | succ m => exact absurd hv (by omega)
    44вҶ’  Hint "Now classify `f 0` and `f 1`:
    45вҶ’  `have h0 := classify (f 0)` and `have h1 := classify (f 1)`."
    46вҶ’  have h0 := classify (f 0)
    47вҶ’  have h1 := classify (f 1)
    48вҶ’  Hint "Case-split on `h0` and `h1`. If both are the same value,
    49вҶ’  you have a collision at indices 0 and 1. If they differ, check `f 2`."
    50вҶ’  Hint (hidden := true) "`cases h0 with | inl h0 => ... | inr h0 => ...`
    51вҶ’  then inside each branch, `cases h1 with | inl h1 => ... | inr h1 => ...`"
    52вҶ’  cases h0 with
    53вҶ’  | inl h0 =>
    54вҶ’    cases h1 with
    55вҶ’    | inl h1 =>
    56вҶ’      -- f 0 = 0, f 1 = 0: collision at 0 and 1
    57вҶ’      Hint "Both `f 0` and `f 1` map to `0` вҖ” collision at indices 0 and 1.
    58вҶ’      Provide the witnesses and prove the conjunction."
    59вҶ’      Hint (hidden := true) "`use 0, 1; constructor; В· intro h; cases h; В· rw [h0, h1]`"
    60вҶ’      use 0, 1
    61вҶ’      constructor
    62вҶ’      В· intro h; cases h
    63вҶ’      В· rw [h0, h1]
    64вҶ’    | inr h1 =>
    65вҶ’      -- f 0 = 0, f 1 = 1: check f 2
    66вҶ’      Hint "The outputs differ (`f 0 = 0`, `f 1 = 1`), so no collision
    67вҶ’      yet. Check `f 2` вҖ” it must equal `0` or `1`, matching one of them."
    68вҶ’      Hint (hidden := true) "`have h2 := classify (f 2)` then case-split on `h2`."
    69вҶ’      have h2 := classify (f 2)
    70вҶ’      cases h2 with
    71вҶ’      | inl h2 =>
    72вҶ’        Hint (hidden := true) "`f 2 = 0 = f 0`: use `0, 2; constructor; В· intro h; cases h; В· rw [h0, h2]`"
    73вҶ’        use 0, 2
    74вҶ’        constructor
    75вҶ’        В· intro h; cases h
    76вҶ’        В· rw [h0, h2]
    77вҶ’      | inr h2 =>
    78вҶ’        Hint (hidden := true) "`f 2 = 1 = f 1`: use `1, 2; constructor; В· intro h; cases h; В· rw [h1, h2]`"
    79вҶ’        use 1, 2
    80вҶ’        constructor
    81вҶ’        В· intro h; cases h
    82вҶ’        В· rw [h1, h2]
    83вҶ’  | inr h0 =>
    84вҶ’    cases h1 with
    85вҶ’    | inr h1 =>
    86вҶ’      -- f 0 = 1, f 1 = 1: collision at 0 and 1
    87вҶ’      Hint "Both `f 0` and `f 1` map to `1` вҖ” collision at indices 0 and 1."
    88вҶ’      Hint (hidden := true) "`use 0, 1; constructor; В· intro h; cases h; В· rw [h0, h1]`"
    89вҶ’      use 0, 1
    90вҶ’      constructor
    91вҶ’      В· intro h; cases h
    92вҶ’      В· rw [h0, h1]
    93вҶ’    | inl h1 =>
    94вҶ’      -- f 0 = 1, f 1 = 0: check f 2
    95вҶ’      Hint "The outputs differ (`f 0 = 1`, `f 1 = 0`), so no collision
    96вҶ’      yet. Check `f 2` вҖ” it must match one of them."
    97вҶ’      Hint (hidden := true) "`have h2 := classify (f 2)` then case-split on `h2`."
    98вҶ’      have h2 := classify (f 2)
    99вҶ’      cases h2 with
   100вҶ’      | inl h2 =>
   101вҶ’        Hint (hidden := true) "`f 2 = 0 = f 1`: use `1, 2; constructor; В· intro h; cases h; В· rw [h1, h2]`"
   102вҶ’        use 1, 2
   103вҶ’        constructor
   104вҶ’        В· intro h; cases h
   105вҶ’        В· rw [h1, h2]
   106вҶ’      | inr h2 =>
   107вҶ’        Hint (hidden := true) "`f 2 = 1 = f 0`: use `0, 2; constructor; В· intro h; cases h; В· rw [h0, h2]`"
   108вҶ’        use 0, 2
   109вҶ’        constructor
   110вҶ’        В· intro h; cases h
   111вҶ’        В· rw [h0, h2]
   112вҶ’
   113вҶ’Conclusion "
   114вҶ’You've proved the **pigeonhole principle** for `Fin 3 -> Fin 2`:
   115вҶ’any function from a 3-element set to a 2-element set must have
   116вҶ’a collision.
   117вҶ’
   118вҶ’The proof strategy was:
   119вҶ’1. **Classify** each output as `0` or `1`
   120вҶ’2. **Compare** outputs at pairs of inputs
   121вҶ’3. **Find** two inputs with the same output
   122вҶ’

   124вҶ’- Case analysis on `Fin 2` (MeetFin)
   125вҶ’- Existential witnesses with `use` (MeetFin)
   126вҶ’- Inequality pattern `intro h; cases h` (MeetFin)
   127вҶ’- Rewriting with hypotheses (FinTuples)
   128вҶ’
   129вҶ’**The abstract pigeonhole principle**: This generalizes. For any
   130вҶ’function `f : Fin (n+1) -> Fin n`, there must exist `i j` with
   131вҶ’`i /= j` and `f i = f j`. More inputs than outputs forces a
   132вҶ’collision вҖ” always. The concrete proof here used exhaustive case
   133вҶ’analysis, which doesn't scale: for `Fin 100 -> Fin 99`, you'd
   134вҶ’need astronomical case splits. In the Finset worlds ahead, you'll
   135вҶ’learn cardinality tools that prove pigeonhole for any `n` in a
   136вҶ’few lines.
   137вҶ’
   138вҶ’**The cardinality consequence**: If there were a collision-free
   139вҶ’function from `Fin 3` to `Fin 2`, we could embed a 3-element set

   141вҶ’confirming that `Fin 3` is genuinely 'larger than' `Fin 2`. More
   142вҶ’importantly, the parameter `n` in `Fin n` is *recoverable* from
   143вҶ’the type: `Fin n` and `Fin m` can only be equivalent (have a
   144вҶ’bijection between them) when `n = m`. This is what makes `Fin n`
   145вҶ’the canonical finite type вҖ” it uniquely represents 'a type with
   146вҶ’exactly n elements.'
   147вҶ’"

file.content:
import Game.Metadata

World "PsetFin"
Level 14

Title "The Pigeonhole Principle (Concrete)"

Introduction "
# Three Pigeons, Two Holes

The **pigeonhole principle** says: if you put more objects into
fewer boxes, at least two objects share a box.

Formally: any function `f : Fin 3 -> Fin 2` must send two
different inputs to the same output. There are 3 inputs but
only 2 possible outputs, so a collision is inevitable.

This is your first real **theorem from combinatorics** вҖ” and
you can prove it using only the tools from Phase 1.

**Strategy**:
1. Classify each `Fin 2` value: every `x : Fin 2` is either `0` or `1`
2. Apply the classification to `f 0` and `f 1`
3. If they're equal: witnesses are `0` and `1`
4. If they differ: check `f 2` вҖ” it must match one of them
"

/-- Any function from Fin 3 to Fin 2 has a collision. -/
Statement (f : Fin 3 вҶ’ Fin 2) : вҲғ i j : Fin 3, i вү  j вҲ§ f i = f j := by
  -- Helper: every Fin 2 element is 0 or 1
  Hint "First, prove a helper: every element of `Fin 2` is `0` or `1`.
  Use `have classify : forall x : Fin 2, x = 0 or x = 1 := by ...`"
  Hint (hidden := true) "Inside the `have`, destructure:
  `intro (v, hv); cases v with | zero => left; rfl | succ n => ...`
  For `succ n`: `cases n with | zero => right; rfl | succ m => exact absurd hv (by omega)`"
  have classify : вҲҖ x : Fin 2, x = 0 вҲЁ x = 1 := by
    intro вҹЁv, hvвҹ©
    cases v with
    | zero => left; rfl
    | succ n =>
      cases n with
      | zero => right; rfl
      | succ m => exact absurd hv (by omega)
  Hint "Now classify `f 0` and `f 1`:
  `have h0 := classify (f 0)` and `have h1 := classify (f 1)`."
  have h0 := classify (f 0)
  have h1 := classify (f 1)
  Hint "Case-split on `h0` and `h1`. If both are the same value,
  you have a collision at indices 0 and 1. If they differ, check `f 2`."
  Hint (hidden := true) "`cases h0 with | inl h0 => ... | inr h0 => ...`
  then inside each branch, `cases h1 with | inl h1 => ... | inr h1 => ...`"
  cases h0 with
  | inl h0 =>
    cases h1 with
    | inl h1 =>
      -- f 0 = 0, f 1 = 0: collision at 0 and 1
      Hint "Both `f 0` and `f 1` map to `0` вҖ” collision at indices 0 and 1.
      Provide the witnesses and prove the conjunction."
      Hint (hidden := true) "`use 0, 1; constructor; В· intro h; cases h; В· rw [h0, h1]`"
      use 0, 1
      constructor
      В· intro h; cases h
      В· rw [h0, h1]
    | inr h1 =>
      -- f 0 = 0, f 1 = 1: check f 2
      Hint "The outputs differ (`f 0 = 0`, `f 1 = 1`), so no collision
      yet. Check `f 2` вҖ” it must equal `0` or `1`, matching one of them."
      Hint (hidden := true) "`have h2 := classify (f 2)` then case-split on `h2`."
      have h2 := classify (f 2)
      cases h2 with
      | inl h2 =>
        Hint (hidden := true) "`f 2 = 0 = f 0`: use `0, 2; constructor; В· intro h; cases h; В· rw [h0, h2]`"
        use 0, 2
        constructor
        В· intro h; cases h
        В· rw [h0, h2]
      | inr h2 =>
        Hint (hidden := true) "`f 2 = 1 = f 1`: use `1, 2; constructor; В· intro h; cases h; В· rw [h1, h2]`"
        use 1, 2
        constructor
        В· intro h; cases h
        В· rw [h1, h2]
  | inr h0 =>
    cases h1 with
    | inr h1 =>
      -- f 0 = 1, f 1 = 1: collision at 0 and 1
      Hint "Both `f 0` and `f 1` map to `1` вҖ” collision at indices 0 and 1."
      Hint (hidden := true) "`use 0, 1; constructor; В· intro h; cases h; В· rw [h0, h1]`"
      use 0, 1
      constructor
      В· intro h; cases h
      В· rw [h0, h1]
    | inl h1 =>
      -- f 0 = 1, f 1 = 0: check f 2
      Hint "The outputs differ (`f 0 = 1`, `f 1 = 0`), so no collision
      yet. Check `f 2` вҖ” it must match one of them."
      Hint (hidden := true) "`have h2 := classify (f 2)` then case-split on `h2`."
      have h2 := classify (f 2)
      cases h2 with
      | inl h2 =>
        Hint (hidden := true) "`f 2 = 0 = f 1`: use `1, 2; constructor; В· intro h; cases h; В· rw [h1, h2]`"
        use 1, 2
        constructor
        В· intro h; cases h
        В· rw [h1, h2]
      | inr h2 =>
        Hint (hidden := true) "`f 2 = 1 = f 0`: use `0, 2; constructor; В· intro h; cases h; В· rw [h0, h2]`"
        use 0, 2
        constructor
        В· intro h; cases h
        В· rw [h0, h2]

Conclusion "
You've proved the **pigeonhole principle** for `Fin 3 -> Fin 2`:
any function from a 3-element set to a 2-element set must have
a collision.

The proof strategy was:
1. **Classify** each output as `0` or `1`
2. **Compare** outputs at pairs of inputs
3. **Find** two inputs with the same output

Every tool in this proof came from Phase 1:
- Case analysis on `Fin 2` (MeetFin)
- Existential witnesses with `use` (MeetFin)
- Inequality pattern `intro h; cases h` (MeetFin)
- Rewriting with hypotheses (FinTuples)

**The abstract pigeonhole principle**: This generalizes. For any
function `f : Fin (n+1) -> Fin n`, there must exist `i j` with
`i /= j` and `f i = f j`. More inputs than outputs forces a
collision вҖ” always. The concrete proof here used exhaustive case
analysis, which doesn't scale: for `Fin 100 -> Fin 99`, you'd
need astronomical case splits. In the Finset worlds ahead, you'll
learn cardinality tools that prove pigeonhole for any `n` in a
few lines.

**The cardinality consequence**: If there were a collision-free
function from `Fin 3` to `Fin 2`, we could embed a 3-element set
into a 2-element set вҖ” which is impossible. So `|Fin 3| > |Fin 2|`,
confirming that `Fin 3` is genuinely 'larger than' `Fin 2`. More
importantly, the parameter `n` in `Fin n` is *recoverable* from
the type: `Fin n` and `Fin m` can only be equivalent (have a
bijection between them) when `n = m`. This is what makes `Fin n`
the canonical finite type вҖ” it uniquely represents 'a type with
exactly n elements.'
"

DisabledTheorem funext

1вҶ’import Game.Metadata
     2вҶ’
     3вҶ’World "PsetFin"
     4вҶ’Level 15
     5вҶ’
     6вҶ’Title "Boss: Phase 1 Complete"
     7вҶ’
     8вҶ’Introduction "
     9вҶ’# Boss: Integrating All Fin Skills
    10вҶ’
    11вҶ’This boss has two parts that draw on all three lecture worlds.
    12вҶ’
    13вҶ’**Part 1 вҖ” Comparing two tuples**: `f` is described by its head
    14вҶ’and tail, `g` by a pointwise formula. Prove they're equal.
    15вҶ’
    16вҶ’Strategy: first **reconstruct** `f` from head + tail (FinTuples
    17вҶ’Level 19 recipe), then use **ext + case split** to compare
    18вҶ’with `g` index by index.
    19вҶ’
    20вҶ’**Part 2 вҖ” Non-constant tuple**: Prove that `f` has at least two
    21вҶ’different values.
    22вҶ’
    23вҶ’Strategy: provide a witness index where `f` differs from `f 0`.
    24вҶ’
    25вҶ’Moves required:
    26вҶ’- Front reconstruction (`.symm` + `rw ... at`)
    27вҶ’- Function extensionality (`ext`)
    28вҶ’- Case analysis with impossible-branch elimination (`absurd`)
    29вҶ’- Universally quantified rewrite (`rw [hg]`)
    30вҶ’- Existential witness (`use`)
    31вҶ’- Inequality pattern
    32вҶ’"
    33вҶ’
    34вҶ’/-- Comparing reconstructed tuples and finding non-constant entries. -/
    35вҶ’Statement (f g : Fin 3 вҶ’ в„•)
    36вҶ’    (hf0 : f 0 = 2) (hft : Fin.tail f = ![4, 6])
    37вҶ’    (hg : вҲҖ i : Fin 3, g i = 2 * i.val + 2) :
    38вҶ’    f = g вҲ§ вҲғ i : Fin 3, f i вү  f 0 := by
    39вҶ’  -- Step 1: Reconstruct f
    40вҶ’  Hint "First, determine what `f` actually is.
    41вҶ’  Use the front reconstruction pattern:
    42вҶ’  `have hf : f = ![2, 4, 6] := by`
    43вҶ’  then inside, start with `have key := (Fin.cons_self_tail f).symm`."
    44вҶ’  have hf : f = ![2, 4, 6] := by
    45вҶ’    Hint (hidden := true) "`have key := (Fin.cons_self_tail f).symm`
    46вҶ’    gives `key : f = Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f)`.
    47вҶ’    Then `rw [hf0, hft] at key` substitutes the known parts.
    48вҶ’    Finally `exact key`."
    49вҶ’    have key := (Fin.cons_self_tail f).symm
    50вҶ’    rw [hf0, hft] at key
    51вҶ’    exact key
    52вҶ’  Hint "Good! Now `hf : f = ![2, 4, 6]`.
    53вҶ’  Split the conjunction with `constructor`."
    54вҶ’  constructor
    55вҶ’  -- Part 1: f = g
    56вҶ’  В· Hint "Show the functions agree at every index.
    57вҶ’    Use `ext вҹЁv, hvвҹ©`, then `rw [hf, hg]` to substitute both."
    58вҶ’    ext вҹЁv, hvвҹ©
    59вҶ’    Hint (hidden := true) "After `rw [hf, hg]`, the goal at each
    60вҶ’    index reduces to a numerical equality. Case-split on `v`
    61вҶ’    and use `rfl` for each valid case."
    62вҶ’    rw [hf, hg]
    63вҶ’    cases v with
    64вҶ’    | zero => rfl
    65вҶ’    | succ n =>
    66вҶ’      cases n with
    67вҶ’      | zero => rfl
    68вҶ’      | succ m =>
    69вҶ’        cases m with
    70вҶ’        | zero => rfl
    71вҶ’        | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)
    72вҶ’  -- Part 2: f has non-constant values
    73вҶ’  В· Hint "Find an index where `f` differs from `f 0`.
    74вҶ’    Since `f = ![2, 4, 6]`, any index besides 0 works."
    75вҶ’    Hint (hidden := true) "`use вҹЁ1, by omegaвҹ©` provides the witness.
    76вҶ’    Then `rw [hf]` substitutes the known tuple, and the
    77вҶ’    inequality between `4` and `2` closes automatically."
    78вҶ’    use вҹЁ1, by omegaвҹ©
    79вҶ’    rw [hf]
    80вҶ’    intro h; cases h
    81вҶ’
    82вҶ’Conclusion "
    83вҶ’Congratulations вҖ” you've completed **Phase 1: Finite Types**!
    84вҶ’
    85вҶ’Here's everything in your toolkit:
    86вҶ’
    87вҶ’**Construction**: `вҹЁk, by omegaвҹ©`, `use`, `![a, b, c]`, `Fin.cons`, `vecSnoc`
    88вҶ’
    89вҶ’**Extraction**: `.val`, `.isLt`, `Fin.val_last`, `Fin.val_succ`, `Fin.val_castSucc`
    90вҶ’
    91вҶ’**Equality**: `ext` (for Fin or functions), `Fin.ext_iff`, `rfl`
    92вҶ’
    93вҶ’**Inequality**: `intro h; cases h` (literals), `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h; omega` (general)
    94вҶ’
    95вҶ’**Case analysis**: `cases x with | mk v hv =>` + nested `cases v`
    96вҶ’
    97вҶ’**Reconstruction**: `Fin.cons_self_tail.symm` (front), `vecSnoc_self_init.symm` (back)
    98вҶ’
    99вҶ’**Impossible cases**: `exact absurd hv (by omega)`
   100вҶ’
   101вҶ’**Why do we need more than `Fin n -> alpha`?** Tuples are powerful
   102вҶ’вҖ” but they're *ordered* and *indexed*. Not everything in mathematics
   103вҶ’has a natural ordering or an index. The set of prime divisors of 30
   104вҶ’is `(2, 3, 5)` вҖ” but why should 2 come first? The set of elements
   105вҶ’in a group with order dividing 6 has no canonical indexing. These
   106вҶ’are collections defined by a **membership predicate** (`x in S` if
   107вҶ’`P x`), not by position.
   108вҶ’
   109вҶ’Phase 2 introduces `Finset` вҖ” unordered finite collections with
   110вҶ’decidable membership вҖ” to handle exactly this. You'll build finite
   111вҶ’sets, prove membership facts, and compute with set operations. The
   112вҶ’`Fin` skills you've mastered will appear throughout: `Finset`
   113вҶ’membership proofs often reduce to `Fin`-level reasoning.
   114вҶ’
   115вҶ’The most direct bridge: `Finset.range n = {0, 1, ..., n-1}` is
   116вҶ’essentially `Fin n` repackaged as an unordered set. Where `Fin n`
   117вҶ’gives you *indexed access* (`f i` for `i : Fin n`), `Finset.range n`
   118вҶ’gives you *membership* (`k in Finset.range n iff k < n`). Same
   119вҶ’elements, different interface вҖ” and you'll see how the `Fin`-level
   120вҶ’reasoning you've mastered transfers directly to the `Finset` world.
   121вҶ’"

import Game.Metadata

World "PsetFin"
Level 15

Title "Boss: Phase 1 Complete"

Introduction "
# Boss: Integrating All Fin Skills

This boss has two parts that draw on all three lecture worlds.

**Part 1 вҖ” Comparing two tuples**: `f` is described by its head
and tail, `g` by a pointwise formula. Prove they're equal.

Strategy: first **reconstruct** `f` from head + tail (FinTuples
Level 19 recipe), then use **ext + case split** to compare
with `g` index by index.

**Part 2 вҖ” Non-constant tuple**: Prove that `f` has at least two
different values.

Strategy: provide a witness index where `f` differs from `f 0`.

Moves required:
- Front reconstruction (`.symm` + `rw ... at`)
- Function extensionality (`ext`)
- Case analysis with impossible-branch elimination (`absurd`)
- Universally quantified rewrite (`rw [hg]`)
- Existential witness (`use`)
- Inequality pattern
"

/-- Comparing reconstructed tuples and finding non-constant entries. -/
Statement (f g : Fin 3 вҶ’ в„•)
    (hf0 : f 0 = 2) (hft : Fin.tail f = ![4, 6])
    (hg : вҲҖ i : Fin 3, g i = 2 * i.val + 2) :
    f = g вҲ§ вҲғ i : Fin 3, f i вү  f 0 := by
  -- Step 1: Reconstruct f
  Hint "First, determine what `f` actually is.
  Use the front reconstruction pattern:
  `have hf : f = ![2, 4, 6] := by`
  then inside, start with `have key := (Fin.cons_self_tail f).symm`."
  have hf : f = ![2, 4, 6] := by
    Hint (hidden := true) "`have key := (Fin.cons_self_tail f).symm`
    gives `key : f = Fin.cons (f 0) (Fin.tail f)`.
    Then `rw [hf0, hft] at key` substitutes the known parts.
    Finally `exact key`."
    have key := (Fin.cons_self_tail f).symm
    rw [hf0, hft] at key
    exact key
  Hint "Good! Now `hf : f = ![2, 4, 6]`.
  Split the conjunction with `constructor`."
  constructor
  -- Part 1: f = g
  В· Hint "Show the functions agree at every index.
    Use `ext вҹЁv, hvвҹ©`, then `rw [hf, hg]` to substitute both."
    ext вҹЁv, hvвҹ©
    Hint (hidden := true) "After `rw [hf, hg]`, the goal at each
    index reduces to a numerical equality. Case-split on `v`
    and use `rfl` for each valid case."
    rw [hf, hg]
    cases v with
    | zero => rfl
    | succ n =>
      cases n with
      | zero => rfl
      | succ m =>
        cases m with
        | zero => rfl
        | succ k => exact absurd hv (by omega)
  -- Part 2: f has non-constant values
  В· Hint "Find an index where `f` differs from `f 0`.
    Since `f = ![2, 4, 6]`, any index besides 0 works."
    Hint (hidden := true) "`use вҹЁ1, by omegaвҹ©` provides the witness.
    Then `rw [hf]` substitutes the known tuple, and the
    inequality between `4` and `2` closes automatically."
    use вҹЁ1, by omegaвҹ©
    rw [hf]
    intro h; cases h

Conclusion "
Congratulations вҖ” you've completed **Phase 1: Finite Types**!

Here's everything in your toolkit:

**Construction**: `вҹЁk, by omegaвҹ©`, `use`, `![a, b, c]`, `Fin.cons`, `vecSnoc`

**Extraction**: `.val`, `.isLt`, `Fin.val_last`, `Fin.val_succ`, `Fin.val_castSucc`

**Equality**: `ext` (for Fin or functions), `Fin.ext_iff`, `rfl`

**Inequality**: `intro h; cases h` (literals), `rw [Fin.ext_iff] at h; omega` (general)

**Case analysis**: `cases x with | mk v hv =>` + nested `cases v`

**Reconstruction**: `Fin.cons_self_tail.symm` (front), `vecSnoc_self_init.symm` (back)

**Impossible cases**: `exact absurd hv (by omega)`

**Why do we need more than `Fin n -> alpha`?** Tuples are powerful
вҖ” but they're *ordered* and *indexed*. Not everything in mathematics
has a natural ordering or an index. The set of prime divisors of 30
is `(2, 3, 5)` вҖ” but why should 2 come first? The set of elements
in a group with order dividing 6 has no canonical indexing. These
are collections defined by a **membership predicate** (`x in S` if
`P x`), not by position.

Phase 2 introduces `Finset` вҖ” unordered finite collections with
decidable membership вҖ” to handle exactly this. You'll build finite
sets, prove membership facts, and compute with set operations. The
`Fin` skills you've mastered will appear throughout: `Finset`
membership proofs often reduce to `Fin`-level reasoning.

The most direct bridge: `Finset.range n = {0, 1, ..., n-1}` is
essentially `Fin n` repackaged as an unordered set. Where `Fin n`
gives you *indexed access* (`f i` for `i : Fin n`), `Finset.range n`
gives you *membership* (`k in Finset.range n iff k < n`). Same
elements, different interface вҖ” and you'll see how the `Fin`-level
reasoning you've mastered transfers directly to the `Finset` world.
"

DisabledTactic trivial decide native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num by_cases
DisabledTheorem funext

    10вҶ’

import Game.Metadata

World "FinsetBasics"
Level 9

Title "The Fin-to-Finset Bridge"

Introduction "
# Connecting Fin to Finset.range

In Phase 1, every element `i : Fin n` carried a proof `i.isLt : i.val < n`.
In Levels 7-8, you proved that `i вҲҲ Finset.range n вҶ” i < n`.

These are the same condition! Every `Fin n` element's value is automatically
in `Finset.range n`. This is the concrete bridge between Phase 1 (indexed
access) and Phase 2 (set membership).

**Your task**: Prove that for any `i : Fin n`, the value `i.val` belongs
to `Finset.range n`.

**Strategy**: Use `rw [Finset.mem_range]` to convert to `i.val < n`,
then use `exact i.isLt` вҖ” the bound proof that every `Fin n` element
carries.
"

/-- Every Fin n element's value belongs to Finset.range n. -/
Statement (n : в„•) (i : Fin n) : i.val вҲҲ Finset.range n := by
  Hint "Convert the membership goal to an inequality:
  `rw [Finset.mem_range]`."
  rw [Finset.mem_range]
  Hint "The goal is `i.val < n`. Every element of `Fin n` carries
  exactly this proof вҖ” it's called `i.isLt`. Use `exact i.isLt`."
  exact i.isLt

Conclusion "
This level makes the Phase 1 вҶ’ Phase 2 connection concrete:

| Phase 1 (Fin n) | Phase 2 (Finset.range n) |
|---|---|
| `i : Fin n` | `i.val вҲҲ Finset.range n` |
| `i.isLt : i.val < n` | `mem_range` converts to `i.val < n` |
| Ordered index access | Unordered set membership |

The bound proof `i.isLt` is the bridge: it's the proof that `Fin n`
carries internally, and it's exactly what `Finset.mem_range` requires.

This is the forward direction of the bridge. In the next level,
you'll prove the **converse**: given `k вҲҲ Finset.range n`, you
can construct a `Fin n` element with value `k`. Together, these
two levels establish a complete equivalence between `Fin n` and
`Finset.range n`.

When you encounter `Finset.range n` in later worlds вҖ” especially in
summation (`вҲ‘ i вҲҲ Finset.range n, f i`) вҖ” remember that its elements
are exactly the values that `Fin n` indices can take.
"

DisabledTactic trivial В«decideВ» native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num by_cases tauto

1вҶ’import Game.Metadata
     2вҶ’
     3вҶ’World "FinsetBasics"
     4вҶ’Level 18
     5вҶ’
     6вҶ’Title "Boss: Finset Fundamentals"
     7вҶ’
     8вҶ’Introduction "
     9вҶ’# Boss: Finset Fundamentals
    10вҶ’
    11вҶ’Time to combine everything. You're given a finset `s` that equals
    12вҶ’`{2, 4, 6}`, and you must prove five facts about it:
    13вҶ’
    14вҶ’1. **Membership**: `4 вҲҲ s` вҖ” peel inserts to find 4
    15вҶ’2. **Non-membership**: `5 вҲү s` вҖ” assume membership, derive contradiction
    16вҶ’3. **Range coverage**: `вҲҖ x вҲҲ s, x вҲҲ Finset.range 7` вҖ” every element of `s` is in `range 7`
    17вҶ’4. **Nonempty**: `s.Nonempty` вҖ” provide a witness element
    18вҶ’5. **Universal idempotency**: `вҲҖ x вҲҲ s, insert x s = s` вҖ” combine intro with apply-then-prove
    19вҶ’
    20вҶ’Most parts start with `rw [hs]` to substitute the known value of `s`.
    21вҶ’Part 3 combines multiple skills: substitution, intro, reading membership
    22вҶ’backward, and range membership. Part 5 combines universally quantified
    23вҶ’intro with the apply-then-prove pattern from Level 14.
    24вҶ’"
    25вҶ’
    26вҶ’/-- Five fundamental facts about finsets. -/
    27вҶ’Statement (s : Finset в„•) (hs : s = {2, 4, 6}) :
    28вҶ’    (4 вҲҲ s) вҲ§ (5 вҲү s) вҲ§ (вҲҖ x вҲҲ s, x вҲҲ Finset.range 7) вҲ§ s.Nonempty вҲ§ (вҲҖ x вҲҲ s, insert x s = s) := by
    29вҶ’  Hint "The goal is a five-part conjunction. Use `constructor` to split
    30вҶ’  off the first part."
    31вҶ’  constructor
    32вҶ’  -- Part 1: Membership
    33вҶ’  В· Hint "Substitute `s`: `rw [hs]`. Then prove membership of `4`
    34вҶ’    by peeling inserts."
    35вҶ’    rw [hs]
    36вҶ’    Hint (hidden := true) "Chain the rewrites:
    37вҶ’    `rw [Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_singleton]`
    38вҶ’    gives a three-way disjunction. Then `right; left; rfl`."
    39вҶ’    rw [Finset.mem_insert]
    40вҶ’    right
    41вҶ’    rw [Finset.mem_insert]
    42вҶ’    left
    43вҶ’    rfl
    44вҶ’  Hint "The remaining goal is still a conjunction. Use `constructor`
    45вҶ’  to split off the next part."
    46вҶ’  constructor
    47вҶ’  -- Part 2: Non-membership
    48вҶ’  В· Hint "Substitute `s`: `rw [hs]`. Then prove non-membership of `5`."
    49вҶ’    rw [hs]
    50вҶ’    Hint (hidden := true) "`intro h`, then
    51вҶ’    `rw [Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_singleton] at h`,
    52вҶ’    then `omega`."
    53вҶ’    intro h
    54вҶ’    rw [Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_singleton] at h
    55вҶ’    omega
    56вҶ’  Hint "Another conjunction remains. Use `constructor` again."
    57вҶ’  constructor
    58вҶ’  -- Part 3: Range coverage (all elements of s are in range 7)
    59вҶ’  В· Hint "Substitute `s`: `rw [hs]`. Then introduce `x` and its
    60вҶ’    membership hypothesis with `intro x hx`."
    61вҶ’    rw [hs]
    62вҶ’    Hint (hidden := true) "After `intro x hx`, unfold `hx` with
    63вҶ’    `rw [Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_singleton] at hx`,
    64вҶ’    then `rw [Finset.mem_range]; omega`."
    65вҶ’    intro x hx
    66вҶ’    rw [Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_singleton] at hx
    67вҶ’    rw [Finset.mem_range]
    68вҶ’    omega
    69вҶ’  Hint "Two parts left. Use `constructor` to split them."
    70вҶ’  constructor
    71вҶ’  -- Part 4: Nonempty
    72вҶ’  В· Hint "Substitute `s`: `rw [hs]`. Then prove `Nonempty` by
    73вҶ’    providing a witness with `use`."
    74вҶ’    rw [hs]
    75вҶ’    Hint (hidden := true) "`use 2; rw [Finset.mem_insert]; left; rfl`
    76вҶ’    provides 2 as the witness and proves its membership."
    77вҶ’    use 2
    78вҶ’    rw [Finset.mem_insert]
    79вҶ’    left
    80вҶ’    rfl
    81вҶ’  -- Part 5: Universal insert idempotency
    82вҶ’  В· Hint "Substitute `s`: `rw [hs]`. Then introduce `x` and its
    83вҶ’    membership hypothesis with `intro x hx`."
    84вҶ’    rw [hs]
    85вҶ’    Hint (hidden := true) "After `intro x hx`, use
    86вҶ’    `apply Finset.insert_eq_of_mem` to reduce to a membership proof,
    87вҶ’    then `exact hx`."
    88вҶ’    intro x hx
    89вҶ’    Hint "Now use the apply-then-prove pattern:
    90вҶ’    `apply Finset.insert_eq_of_mem` reduces the equation to membership."
    91вҶ’    apply Finset.insert_eq_of_mem
    92вҶ’    Hint (hidden := true) "The goal is membership of `x` in the
    93вҶ’    concrete set вҖ” but you already have `{hx}`. Use `exact {hx}`."
    94вҶ’    exact hx
    95вҶ’
    96вҶ’Conclusion "
    97вҶ’Congratulations вҖ” you've completed **Finset Basics**!
    98вҶ’
    99вҶ’Your finset toolkit:
   100вҶ’
   101вҶ’| Move | Tool | Learned in |
   102вҶ’|---|---|---|
   103вҶ’| Prove `a вҲҲ insert b s` | `rw [Finset.mem_insert]` then `left`/`right` | Level 1 |
   104вҶ’| Prove `a вҲҲ {{b}}` (singleton) | `rw [Finset.mem_singleton]` | Level 2 |
   105вҶ’| Prove `a вҲү вҲ…` | `exact Finset.notMem_empty a` | Level 3 |
   106вҶ’| Prove `m вҲҲ Finset.range n` | `rw [Finset.mem_range]; omega` | Level 4-8 |
   107вҶ’| Prove `m вҲү Finset.range n` | `intro h; rw [Finset.mem_range] at h; omega` | Level 5, 11 |
   108вҶ’| Prove range 0 is empty | non-membership for all elements | Level 6 |
   109вҶ’| Bridge `Fin n` to `Finset.range n` | `rw [Finset.mem_range]; exact i.isLt` | Level 9-10 |
   110вҶ’| Prove `a вҲү {{b, c, d}}` | `intro h; rw [mem_insert, ...] at h; omega` | Level 12 |
   111вҶ’| Unfold `h : x вҲҲ {{a, b, c}}` | `rw [mem_insert, ...] at h` | Level 13 |
   112вҶ’| Prove `insert a s = s` | `apply Finset.insert_eq_of_mem` (apply-then-prove) | Level 14 |
   113вҶ’| Extract from `s.Nonempty` | `obtain вҹЁx, hxвҹ© := hs` | Level 15 |
   114вҶ’| Prove `s.Nonempty` | `use a` then prove `a вҲҲ s` | Level 16 |
   115вҶ’| Close concrete goals | `decide` | Level 17 |
   116вҶ’
   117вҶ’**One thing this world can't do yet**: prove that two finsets are
   118вҶ’**equal**. You showed membership and non-membership, but proving
   119вҶ’`{{1, 2, 3}} = {{3, 1, 2}}` (same elements, different order) requires
   120вҶ’showing every element of one is in the other. That's the job of the
   121вҶ’`ext` tactic, which you'll learn in the FinsetOperations world.
   122вҶ’
   123вҶ’**Also missing**: you can prove *what's in* a finset, but not
   124вҶ’*how many* elements it has. The number of elements is `s.card`
   125вҶ’(the **cardinality**), and you'll learn to compute it in the
   126вҶ’Cardinality world.
   127вҶ’
   128вҶ’**What's next**: The next world introduces *operations* on finsets вҖ”
   129вҶ’union (`вҲӘ`), intersection (`вҲ©`), set difference (`\\`), and the `ext`
   130вҶ’tactic for proving two finsets are equal. These operations will let
   131вҶ’you build new finsets from old ones, and `ext` will let you prove
   132вҶ’equalities by showing they have the same elements.
   133вҶ’"
   134вҶ’
   135вҶ’DisabledTactic trivial В«decideВ» native_decide simp aesop simp_all fin_cases interval_cases norm_num by_cases tauto
   136вҶ’DisabledTheorem Finset.mem_insert_self Finset.mem_insert_of_mem Finset.insert_nonempty Finset.singleton_nonempty Finset.not_mem_range_self
   137вҶ’

    `rw [Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_singleton] at h`,

    provides 2 as the witness and proves its membership."
    use 2
    rw [Finset.mem_insert]
    left
    rfl
  -- Part 5: Universal insert idempotency
  В· Hint "Substitute `s`: `rw [hs]`. Then introduce `x` and its
    membership hypothesis with `intro x hx`."
    rw [hs]
    Hint (hidden := true) "After `intro x hx`, use
    `apply Finset.insert_eq_of_mem` to reduce to a membership proof,
    then `exact hx`."
    intro x hx
    Hint "Now use the apply-then-prove pattern:
    `apply Finset.insert_eq_of_mem` reduces the equation to membership."
    apply Finset.insert_eq_of_mem
    Hint (hidden := true) "The goal is membership of `x` in the
    concrete set вҖ” but you already have `{hx}`. Use `exact {hx}`."
    exact hx

Conclusion "
Congratulations вҖ” you've completed **Finset Basics**!

Your finset toolkit:

| Move | Tool | Learned in |
|---|---|---|
| Prove `a вҲҲ insert b s` | `rw [Finset.mem_insert]` then `left`/`right` | Level 1 |
| Prove `a вҲҲ {{b}}` (singleton) | `rw [Finset.mem_singleton]` | Level 2 |
| Prove `a вҲү вҲ…` | `exact Finset.notMem_empty a` | Level 3 |
| Prove `m вҲҲ Finset.range n` | `rw [Finset.mem_range]; omega` | Level 4-8 |
| Prove `m вҲү Finset.range n` | `intro h; rw [Finset.mem_range] at h; omega` | Level 5, 11 |
| Prove range 0 is empty | non-membership for all elements | Level 6 |
| Bridge `Fin n` to `Finset.range n` | `rw [Finset.mem_range]; exact i.isLt` | Level 9-10 |
| Prove `a вҲү {{b, c, d}}` | `intro h; rw [mem_insert, ...] at h; omega` | Level 12 |
| Unfold `h : x вҲҲ {{a, b, c}}` | `rw [mem_insert, ...] at h` | Level 13 |
| Prove `insert a s = s` | `apply Finset.insert_eq_of_mem` (apply-then-prove) | Level 14 |
| Extract from `s.Nonempty` | `obtain вҹЁx, hxвҹ© := hs` | Level 15 |
| Prove `s.Nonempty` | `use a` then prove `a вҲҲ s` | Level 16 |
| Close concrete goals | `decide` | Level 17 |

**One thing this world can't do yet**: prove that two finsets are
**equal**. You showed membership and non-membership, but proving
`{{1, 2, 3}} = {{3, 1, 2}}` (same elements, different order) requires
showing every element of one is in the other. That's the job of the
`ext` tactic, which you'll learn in the FinsetOperations world.

**Also missing**: you can prove *what's in* a finset, but not
*how many* elements it has. The number of elements is `s.card`
(the **cardinality**), and you'll learn to compute it in the
Cardinality world.

**What's next**: The next world introduces *operations* on finsets вҖ”
union (`вҲӘ`), intersection (`вҲ©`), set difference (`\\`), and the `ext`
tactic for proving two finsets are equal. These operations will let
you build new finsets from old ones, and `ext` will let you prove
equalities by showing they have the same elements.
"

file.content:
import Game.Metadata

World "FinsetOperations"
Level 16

Title "Lattice Notation"

Introduction "
# New Tactics: `show` and `change`

Before tackling the main topic, you need two new tactics:

**`show`** replaces the current goal with a definitionally equal
statement. If the goal displays as `x вҲҲ s вҠ” t`, you can write
`show x вҲҲ s вҲӘ t` and Lean accepts it because `вҠ”` and `вҲӘ` are
the same operation. The goal's *meaning* is unchanged; only the
*notation* changes.

**`change ... at h`** does the same for a hypothesis. If
`h : x вҲҲ s вҠ“ t`, then `change x вҲҲ s вҲ© t at h` rewrites the
hypothesis to use `вҲ©` notation instead.

Both tactics work whenever the old and new expressions are
*definitionally equal* вҖ” the same value, just written differently.

# Lattice Notation: вҠ” and вҠ“

In Lean, finset union and intersection are implemented through the
**lattice** structure. This means you'll sometimes see alternative
notation in the infoview:

- `s вҠ” t` is the same as `s вҲӘ t` (lattice 'supremum' = union)
- `s вҠ“ t` is the same as `s вҲ© t` (lattice 'infimum' = intersection)

These are **exactly the same operations** вҖ” just different notation.

**The problem**: `rw [Finset.mem_union]` matches `вҲӘ` but not `вҠ”`.
When lattice notation appears, you need to convert it first using
`show` (for the goal) or `change` (for hypotheses). After
converting, all the familiar membership lemmas work normally.

You may also encounter `вүӨ` in place of `вҠҶ` (lattice order = subset).
The same `show`/`change` technique handles it.

**Your task**: Given `x вҲҲ s вҠ“ t` (intersection via lattice notation),
prove `x вҲҲ s вҠ” t` (union via lattice notation).
"

/-- From intersection to union via lattice notation. -/
Statement (s t : Finset в„•) (x : в„•) (h : x вҲҲ s вҠ“ t) : x вҲҲ s вҠ” t := by
  Hint "The goal uses `вҠ”` (lattice supremum = union). Convert it
  to familiar notation with `show x вҲҲ s вҲӘ t`."
  show x вҲҲ s вҲӘ t
  Hint "The hypothesis uses `вҠ“` (lattice infimum = intersection).
  Convert it with `change x вҲҲ s вҲ© t at h`."
  change x вҲҲ s вҲ© t at h
  Hint "Now both goal and hypothesis use familiar notation.
  Use `rw [Finset.mem_inter] at h` and `rw [Finset.mem_union]`."
  rw [Finset.mem_inter] at h
  rw [Finset.mem_union]
  Hint (hidden := true) "Choose `left` and use `exact h.1`."
  left
  exact h.1

Conclusion "
The key insight: `вҠ”` = `вҲӘ` and `вҠ“` = `вҲ©` for finsets, but `rw`
can't see through the notation difference. Use `show` (goal) or
`change` (hypothesis) to convert lattice notation to finset notation
before rewriting.

The workflow when you encounter lattice notation:
1. **Recognize** `вҠ”` as union and `вҠ“` as intersection
2. **Convert** with `show`/`change` to familiar notation
3. **Proceed** with `rw [Finset.mem_union]` etc. as usual

In the next level, you'll learn `simp only` вҖ” a tool for unfolding
multiple membership layers at once.
"

NewTactic В«showВ» В«changeВ»

1вҶ’import Game.Metadata
     2вҶ’
     3вҶ’World "FinsetOperations"
     4вҶ’Level 24
     5вҶ’
     6вҶ’Title "Boss: De Morgan's Second Law"
     7вҶ’
     8вҶ’Introduction "
     9вҶ’# Boss: Set Difference Distributes Over Intersection
    10вҶ’
    11вҶ’Time to combine everything from this world. You'll prove a set identity
    12вҶ’that integrates the unfold-to-logic recipe with set difference and
    13вҶ’proof by contradiction:
    14вҶ’
    15вҶ’$$s \\setminus (t \\cap u) = (s \\setminus t) \\cup (s \\setminus u)$$
    16вҶ’
    17вҶ’This is **De Morgan's second law** for sets: removing elements in
    18вҶ’*both* `t` and `u` is the same as taking elements removed from `t`
    19вҶ’OR removed from `u`.
    20вҶ’
    21вҶ’Compare with Level 17 (De Morgan's first law):
    22вҶ’$s \\setminus (t \\cup u) = (s \\setminus t) \\cap (s \\setminus u)$.
    23вҶ’The first law swaps union to intersection; this one swaps intersection
    24вҶ’to union.
    25вҶ’
    26вҶ’After `ext x; simp only [Finset.mem_sdiff, Finset.mem_inter, Finset.mem_union]`,
    27вҶ’the goal becomes:
    28вҶ’
    29вҶ’$$x \\in s \\land \\neg(x \\in t \\land x \\in u) \\;\\longleftrightarrow\\; (x \\in s \\land x \\notin t) \\lor (x \\in s \\land x \\notin u)$$
    30вҶ’
    31вҶ’The **backward direction** is straightforward: from either side of the
    32вҶ’disjunction, extract `x \\in s` and derive the negated conjunction by
    33вҶ’applying the non-membership to a component.
    34вҶ’
    35вҶ’The **forward direction** is the challenge. You have `x \\in s` and
    36вҶ’`\\neg(x \\in t \\land x \\in u)`, but you need to produce `x \\notin t`
    37вҶ’or `x \\notin u` вҖ” positive non-membership information from a negated
    38вҶ’conjunction. This requires `by_contra`:
    39вҶ’
    40вҶ’1. Assume the goal is false (neither disjunct holds)
    41вҶ’2. Show that this forces both `x \\in t` and `x \\in u`
    42вҶ’3. Contradiction with `\\neg(x \\in t \\land x \\in u)`
    43вҶ’
    44вҶ’The skill integration:
    45вҶ’- **ext** (Level 8): convert set equality to logic
    46вҶ’- **mem_sdiff, mem_inter, mem_union** (Levels 1-3): convert to logic
    47вҶ’- **simp only** (Level 16): unfold all at once
    48вҶ’- **by_contra** (Level 18): assume the negation and derive contradiction
    49вҶ’- **have** (from NNG4): build intermediate results
    50вҶ’- **constructor, cases, .1, .2, left, right**: standard logic handling
    51вҶ’"
    52вҶ’
    53вҶ’/-- De Morgan's second law for sets. -/
    54вҶ’Statement (s t u : Finset в„•) : s \ (t вҲ© u) = (s \ t) вҲӘ (s \ u) := by
    55вҶ’  Hint "Apply the unfold-to-logic recipe: `ext x`, then
    56вҶ’  `simp only [Finset.mem_sdiff, Finset.mem_inter, Finset.mem_union]`."
    57вҶ’  ext x
    58вҶ’  simp only [Finset.mem_sdiff, Finset.mem_inter, Finset.mem_union]
    59вҶ’  Hint "The goal is
    60вҶ’  `x вҲҲ s вҲ§ В¬(x вҲҲ t вҲ§ x вҲҲ u) вҶ” (x вҲҲ s вҲ§ x вҲү t) вҲЁ (x вҲҲ s вҲ§ x вҲү u)`.
    61вҶ’  Use `constructor` to split the biconditional."
    62вҶ’  constructor
    63вҶ’  -- Forward: x вҲҲ s вҲ§ В¬(x вҲҲ t вҲ§ x вҲҲ u) вҶ’ (x вҲҲ s вҲ§ x вҲү t) вҲЁ (x вҲҲ s вҲ§ x вҲү u)
    64вҶ’  В· Hint "Use `intro h`. Then `h.1 : x вҲҲ s` and
    65вҶ’    `h.2 : В¬(x вҲҲ t вҲ§ x вҲҲ u)`. You need to produce
    66вҶ’    `(x вҲҲ s вҲ§ x вҲү t) вҲЁ (x вҲҲ s вҲ§ x вҲү u)`.
    67вҶ’
    68вҶ’    The challenge: you know `x вҲҲ t` and `x вҲҲ u` can't BOTH hold,
    69вҶ’    but you don't know which one fails. Use `by_contra` to assume
    70вҶ’    the whole goal is false, then show both must hold вҖ” contradiction."
    71вҶ’    intro h
    72вҶ’    Hint (hidden := true) "Use `by_contra h_neg`. This gives
    73вҶ’    `h_neg : В¬((x вҲҲ s вҲ§ x вҲү t) вҲЁ (x вҲҲ s вҲ§ x вҲү u))` and changes
    74вҶ’    the goal to `False`.
    75вҶ’
    76вҶ’    Strategy: show `x вҲҲ t` (if not, the left disjunct holds,
    77вҶ’    contradicting h_neg). Similarly show `x вҲҲ u`. Then
    78вҶ’    `h.2 (And.intro ht hu)` gives `False`."
    79вҶ’    by_contra h_neg
    80вҶ’    Hint "The goal is `False`. You need to show that `h.2` and `h_neg`
    81вҶ’    are contradictory.
    82вҶ’
    83вҶ’    Use `apply h.2` to change the goal to `x вҲҲ t вҲ§ x вҲҲ u` вҖ” if you
    84вҶ’    can build that, `h.2` derives `False`."
    85вҶ’    apply h.2
    86вҶ’    Hint "The goal is `x вҲҲ t вҲ§ x вҲҲ u`. Use `constructor` to split it."
    87вҶ’    constructor
    88вҶ’    -- Prove x вҲҲ t
    89вҶ’    В· Hint "The goal is `x вҲҲ t`. Use `by_contra hnt` to assume `x вҲү t`
    90вҶ’      and derive a contradiction with `h_neg`."
    91вҶ’      by_contra hnt
    92вҶ’      Hint (hidden := true) "You have `hnt : x вҲү t`. Then
    93вҶ’      `вҹЁh.1, hntвҹ© : x вҲҲ s вҲ§ x вҲү t`, so
    94вҶ’      `Or.inl вҹЁh.1, hntвҹ© : (x вҲҲ s вҲ§ x вҲү t) вҲЁ (x вҲҲ s вҲ§ x вҲү u)`.
    95вҶ’      This contradicts `h_neg`.
    96вҶ’      Use `apply h_neg; left; exact вҹЁh.1, hntвҹ©`."
    97вҶ’      apply h_neg
    98вҶ’      left
    99вҶ’      exact вҹЁh.1, hntвҹ©
   100вҶ’    -- Prove x вҲҲ u
   101вҶ’    В· Hint "Same pattern: `by_contra hnu` then derive a contradiction."
   102вҶ’      by_contra hnu
   103вҶ’      Hint (hidden := true) "`apply h_neg; right; exact вҹЁh.1, hnuвҹ©`."
   104вҶ’      apply h_neg
   105вҶ’      right
   106вҶ’      exact вҹЁh.1, hnuвҹ©
   107вҶ’  -- Backward: (x вҲҲ s вҲ§ x вҲү t) вҲЁ (x вҲҲ s вҲ§ x вҲү u) вҶ’ x вҲҲ s вҲ§ В¬(x вҲҲ t вҲ§ x вҲҲ u)
   108вҶ’  В· Hint "Use `intro h` then `cases h with` to split the disjunction."
   109вҶ’    intro h
   110вҶ’    Hint (hidden := true) "In each case, build the conjunction:
   111вҶ’    `h.1` gives `x вҲҲ s`, and for `В¬(x вҲҲ t вҲ§ x вҲҲ u)`, use
   112вҶ’    `intro htu` and apply the non-membership to a component of `htu`."
   113вҶ’    cases h with
   114вҶ’    | inl hst =>
   115вҶ’      Hint (hidden := true) "You have `hst.1 : x вҲҲ s` and `hst.2 : x вҲү t`.
   116вҶ’      Use `constructor`. First part: `exact hst.1`.
   117вҶ’      Second part: `intro htu; exact hst.2 htu.1`."
   118вҶ’      constructor
   119вҶ’      В· exact hst.1
   120вҶ’      В· intro htu
   121вҶ’        exact hst.2 htu.1
   122вҶ’    | inr hsu =>
   123вҶ’      Hint (hidden := true) "You have `hsu.1 : x вҲҲ s` and `hsu.2 : x вҲү u`.
   124вҶ’      Use `constructor`. First part: `exact hsu.1`.
   125вҶ’      Second part: `intro htu; exact hsu.2 htu.2`."
   126вҶ’      constructor
   127вҶ’      В· exact hsu.1
   128вҶ’      В· intro htu
   129вҶ’        exact hsu.2 htu.2
   130вҶ’
   131вҶ’Conclusion "
   132вҶ’Congratulations вҖ” you've completed **Finset Operations**!
   133вҶ’
   134вҶ’You proved De Morgan's second law for sets:
   135вҶ’$s \\setminus (t \\cap u) = (s \\setminus t) \\cup (s \\setminus u)$.
   136вҶ’
   137вҶ’The forward direction required the most sophisticated use of
   138вҶ’`by_contra` yet: you assumed the *entire disjunction* was false,
   139вҶ’then showed this forces both `x \\in t` and `x \\in u`, contradicting
   140вҶ’the negated conjunction. Each component (`x \\in t`, `x \\in u`) was
   141вҶ’itself proven by contradiction вҖ” a nested use of `by_contra`.
   142вҶ’
   143вҶ’Together with Level 17 (De Morgan's first law), you've proven both
   144вҶ’De Morgan laws for sets:
   145вҶ’- $s \\setminus (t \\cup u) = (s \\setminus t) \\cap (s \\setminus u)$
   146вҶ’- $s \\setminus (t \\cap u) = (s \\setminus t) \\cup (s \\setminus u)$
   147вҶ’
   148вҶ’These mirror the propositional De Morgan laws:
   149вҶ’- $\\neg(P \\lor Q) \\iff \\neg P \\land \\neg Q$
   150вҶ’- $\\neg(P \\land Q) \\iff \\neg P \\lor \\neg Q$
   151вҶ’
   152вҶ’Your finset operations toolkit:
   153вҶ’
   154вҶ’| Operation | Membership lemma | Logic form |
   155вҶ’|---|---|---|
   156вҶ’| `s вҲӘ t` | `Finset.mem_union` | `a вҲҲ s вҲЁ a вҲҲ t` |
   157вҶ’| `s вҲ© t` | `Finset.mem_inter` | `a вҲҲ s вҲ§ a вҲҲ t` |
   158вҶ’| `s \\\\ t` | `Finset.mem_sdiff` | `a вҲҲ s вҲ§ a вҲү t` |
   159вҶ’| `s.filter p` | `Finset.mem_filter` | `a вҲҲ s вҲ§ p a` |

import Game.Metadata

World "FinsetOperations"
Level 24

Title "Boss: De Morgan's Second Law"

Introduction "
# Boss: Set Difference Distributes Over Intersection

Time to combine everything from this world. You'll prove a set identity
that integrates the unfold-to-logic recipe with set difference and
proof by contradiction:

$$s \\setminus (t \\cap u) = (s \\setminus t) \\cup (s \\setminus u)$$

This is **De Morgan's second law** for sets: removing elements in
*both* `t` and `u` is the same as taking elements removed from `t`
OR removed from `u`.

Compare with Level 17 (De Morgan's first law):
$s \\setminus (t \\cup u) = (s \\setminus t) \\cap (s \\setminus u)$.
The first law swaps union to intersection; this one swaps intersection
to union.

After `ext x; simp only [Finset.mem_sdiff, Finset.mem_inter, Finset.mem_union]`,
the goal becomes:

$$x \\in s \\land \\neg(x \\in t \\land x \\in u) \\;\\longleftrightarrow\\; (x \\in s \\land x \\notin t) \\lor (x \\in s \\land x \\notin u)$$

The **backward direction** is straightforward: from either side of the
disjunction, extract `x \\in s` and derive the negated conjunction by
applying the non-membership to a component.

The **forward direction** is the challenge. You have `x \\in s` and
`\\neg(x \\in t \\land x \\in u)`, but you need to produce `x \\notin t`
or `x \\notin u` вҖ” positive non-membership information from a negated
conjunction. This requires `by_contra`:

1. Assume the goal is false (neither disjunct holds)
2. Show that this forces both `x \\in t` and `x \\in u`
3. Contradiction with `\\neg(x \\in t \\land x \\in u)`

The skill integration:
- **ext** (Level 8): convert set equality to logic
- **mem_sdiff, mem_inter, mem_union** (Levels 1-3): convert to logic
- **simp only** (Level 16): unfold all at once
- **by_contra** (Level 18): assume the negation and derive contradiction
- **have** (from NNG4): build intermediate results
- **constructor, cases, .1, .2, left, right**: standard logic handling
"

/-- De Morgan's second law for sets. -/
Statement (s t u : Finset в„•) : s \ (t вҲ© u) = (s \ t) вҲӘ (s \ u) := by
  Hint "Apply the unfold-to-logic recipe: `ext x`, then
  `simp only [Finset.mem_sdiff, Finset.mem_inter, Finset.mem_union]`."
  ext x
  simp only [Finset.mem_sdiff, Finset.mem_inter, Finset.mem_union]
  Hint "The goal is
  `x вҲҲ s вҲ§ В¬(x вҲҲ t вҲ§ x вҲҲ u) вҶ” (x вҲҲ s вҲ§ x вҲү t) вҲЁ (x вҲҲ s вҲ§ x вҲү u)`.
  Use `constructor` to split the biconditional."
  constructor
  -- Forward: x вҲҲ s вҲ§ В¬(x вҲҲ t вҲ§ x вҲҲ u) вҶ’ (x вҲҲ s вҲ§ x вҲү t) вҲЁ (x вҲҲ s вҲ§ x вҲү u)
  В· Hint "Use `intro h`. Then `h.1 : x вҲҲ s` and
    `h.2 : В¬(x вҲҲ t вҲ§ x вҲҲ u)`. You need to produce
    `(x вҲҲ s вҲ§ x вҲү t) вҲЁ (x вҲҲ s вҲ§ x вҲү u)`.

    The challenge: you know `x вҲҲ t` and `x вҲҲ u` can't BOTH hold,
    but you don't know which one fails. Use `by_contra` to assume
    the whole goal is false, then show both must hold вҖ” contradiction."
    intro h
    Hint (hidden := true) "Use `by_contra h_neg`. This gives
    `h_neg : В¬((x вҲҲ s вҲ§ x вҲү t) вҲЁ (x вҲҲ s вҲ§ x вҲү u))` and changes
    the goal to `False`.

    Strategy: show `x вҲҲ t` (if not, the left disjunct holds,
    contradicting h_neg). Similarly show `x вҲҲ u`. Then
    `h.2 (And.intro ht hu)` gives `False`."
    by_contra h_neg
    Hint "The goal is `False`. You need to show that `h.2` and `h_neg`
    are contradictory.

    Use `apply h.2` to change the goal to `x вҲҲ t вҲ§ x вҲҲ u` вҖ” if you
    can build that, `h.2` derives `False`."
    apply h.2
    Hint "The goal is `x вҲҲ t вҲ§ x вҲҲ u`. Use `constructor` to split it."
    constructor
    -- Prove x вҲҲ t
    В· Hint "The goal is `x вҲҲ t`. Use `by_contra hnt` to assume `x вҲү t`
      and derive a contradiction with `h_neg`."
      by_contra hnt
      Hint (hidden := true) "You have `hnt : x вҲү t`. Then
      `вҹЁh.1, hntвҹ© : x вҲҲ s вҲ§ x вҲү t`, so
      `Or.inl вҹЁh.1, hntвҹ© : (x вҲҲ s вҲ§ x вҲү t) вҲЁ (x вҲҲ s вҲ§ x вҲү u)`.
      This contradicts `h_neg`.
      Use `apply h_neg; left; exact вҹЁh.1, hntвҹ©`."
      apply h_neg
      left
      exact вҹЁh.1, hntвҹ©
    -- Prove x вҲҲ u
    В· Hint "Same pattern: `by_contra hnu` then derive a contradiction."
      by_contra hnu
      Hint (hidden := true) "`apply h_neg; right; exact вҹЁh.1, hnuвҹ©`."
      apply h_neg
      right
      exact вҹЁh.1, hnuвҹ©
  -- Backward: (x вҲҲ s вҲ§ x вҲү t) вҲЁ (x вҲҲ s вҲ§ x вҲү u) вҶ’ x вҲҲ s вҲ§ В¬(x вҲҲ t вҲ§ x вҲҲ u)
  В· Hint "Use `intro h` then `cases h with` to split the disjunction."
    intro h
    Hint (hidden := true) "In each case, build the conjunction:
    `h.1` gives `x вҲҲ s`, and for `В¬(x вҲҲ t вҲ§ x вҲҲ u)`, use
    `intro htu` and apply the non-membership to a component of `htu`."
    cases h with
    | inl hst =>
      Hint (hidden := true) "You have `hst.1 : x вҲҲ s` and `hst.2 : x вҲү t`.
      Use `constructor`. First part: `exact hst.1`.
      Second part: `intro htu; exact hst.2 htu.1`."
      constructor
      В· exact hst.1
      В· intro htu
        exact hst.2 htu.1
    | inr hsu =>
      Hint (hidden := true) "You have `hsu.1 : x вҲҲ s` and `hsu.2 : x вҲү u`.
      Use `constructor`. First part: `exact hsu.1`.
      Second part: `intro htu; exact hsu.2 htu.2`."
      constructor
      В· exact hsu.1
      В· intro htu
        exact hsu.2 htu.2

Conclusion "
Congratulations вҖ” you've completed **Finset Operations**!

You proved De Morgan's second law for sets:
$s \\setminus (t \\cap u) = (s \\setminus t) \\cup (s \\setminus u)$.

The forward direction required the most sophisticated use of
`by_contra` yet: you assumed the *entire disjunction* was false,
then showed this forces both `x \\in t` and `x \\in u`, contradicting
the negated conjunction. Each component (`x \\in t`, `x \\in u`) was
itself proven by contradiction вҖ” a nested use of `by_contra`.

Together with Level 17 (De Morgan's first law), you've proven both
De Morgan laws for sets:
- $s \\setminus (t \\cup u) = (s \\setminus t) \\cap (s \\setminus u)$
- $s \\setminus (t \\cap u) = (s \\setminus t) \\cup (s \\setminus u)$

These mirror the propositional De Morgan laws:
- $\\neg(P \\lor Q) \\iff \\neg P \\land \\neg Q$
- $\\neg(P \\land Q) \\iff \\neg P \\lor \\neg Q$

Your finset operations toolkit:

| Operation | Membership lemma | Logic form |
|---|---|---|
| `s вҲӘ t` | `Finset.mem_union` | `a вҲҲ s вҲЁ a вҲҲ t` |
| `s вҲ© t` | `Finset.mem_inter` | `a вҲҲ s вҲ§ a вҲҲ t` |
| `s \\\\ t` | `Finset.mem_sdiff` | `a вҲҲ s вҲ§ a вҲү t` |
| `s.filter p` | `Finset.mem_filter` | `a вҲҲ s вҲ§ p a` |
| `s вҠҶ t` | `Finset.subset_iff` | `вҲҖ x вҲҲ s, x вҲҲ t` |
| `s = t` | `ext` / `Finset.ext_iff` | `вҲҖ x, x вҲҲ s вҶ” x вҲҲ t` |

The proof recipe for any set identity:
1. `ext x` вҖ” reduce to element-wise logic
2. `simp only [mem_union, mem_inter, ...]` вҖ” unfold all operations
3. Handle the resulting вҲ§/вҲЁ/В¬ with `constructor`, `cases`, `left`/`right`
4. When stuck on extracting positive from negative: `by_contra`

**The bigger picture**: Across this world, you've proved commutativity,
associativity, idempotency, identity laws, absorption, distributivity
(both directions), and De Morgan's laws for finset operations. Together
these establish that `Finset` with `вҲӘ`, `вҲ©`, and `\\` forms a
**Boolean algebra** вҖ” a structure with two operations satisfying a
specific set of axioms. `Finset` is the prototypical concrete example.
You'll meet other Boolean algebras (propositions, bit vectors, power
sets) in the algebraic structures course, and the proofs will follow
the same unfold-to-logic recipe you've mastered here.

**What's next**: The next world covers `Finset.image` вҖ” applying a
function to every element of a finset, and the relationship between
injectivity and cardinality of images.
"

1вҶ’import Game.Metadata
     2вҶ’
     3вҶ’World "FinsetImage"
     4вҶ’Level 21
     5вҶ’
     6вҶ’Title "Boss: Image Membership, Bounds, and Injectivity"
     7вҶ’
     8вҶ’Introduction "
     9вҶ’# Boss: Putting It All Together
    10вҶ’
    11вҶ’Consider $f(n) = n + 3$ applied to $S = \\{0, 1, \\ldots, 9\\}$.
    12вҶ’This level integrates three core move families from the world.
    13вҶ’
    14вҶ’**Part 1** вҖ” Forward membership (Levels 1-2):
    15вҶ’Prove $12 \\in f(S)$ by providing the witness $9$.
    16вҶ’
    17вҶ’**Part 2** вҖ” Subset bound via backward extraction (Levels 3-8):
    18вҶ’Prove $f(S) \\subseteq \\{0, \\ldots, 12\\}$. Every element
    19вҶ’$n + 3$ where $n < 10$ satisfies $n + 3 < 13$.
    20вҶ’
    21вҶ’**Part 3** вҖ” Cardinality upper bound (Level 12):
    22вҶ’The image can only shrink: $|f(S)| \\leq |S|$.
    23вҶ’
    24вҶ’**Part 4** вҖ” Exact cardinality via injectivity (Levels 13-14):
    25вҶ’Prove $|f(S)| = |S|$ by proving that $n \\mapsto n+3$ is injective.
    26вҶ’
    27вҶ’No new moves вҖ” only moves seeded in Levels 1-19.
    28вҶ’"
    29вҶ’
    30вҶ’/-- Forward membership, subset bound, cardinality bounds, and injectivity. -/
    31вҶ’Statement :
    32вҶ’    (12 : в„•) вҲҲ (Finset.range 10).image (fun n => n + 3) вҲ§
    33вҶ’    (Finset.range 10).image (fun n => n + 3) вҠҶ Finset.range 13 вҲ§
    34вҶ’    ((Finset.range 10).image (fun n => n + 3)).card вүӨ
    35вҶ’      (Finset.range 10).card вҲ§
    36вҶ’    ((Finset.range 10).image (fun n => n + 3)).card =
    37вҶ’      (Finset.range 10).card := by
    38вҶ’  Hint "Use `constructor` to split into Part 1 and the remaining parts."
    39вҶ’  constructor
    40вҶ’  -- Part 1: 12 вҲҲ image (В· + 3) (range 10)
    41вҶ’  В· Hint "Forward membership: `rw [Finset.mem_image]`, provide the witness.
    42вҶ’    Solve: $n + 3 = 12$ gives $n = 9$."
    43вҶ’    rw [Finset.mem_image]
    44вҶ’    Hint (hidden := true) "Try `use 9`."
    45вҶ’    use 9
    46вҶ’    Hint "Lean verified `9 + 3 = 12`. Remaining: `9 вҲҲ Finset.range 10`.
    47вҶ’    Use `rw [Finset.mem_range]; omega`."
    48вҶ’    rw [Finset.mem_range]
    49вҶ’    omega
    50вҶ’  constructor
    51вҶ’  -- Part 2: image (В· + 3) (range 10) вҠҶ range 13
    52вҶ’  В· Hint "Subset goal: introduce an element with `intro x hx`,
    53вҶ’    then extract the witness from `hx` and show `x < 13`."
    54вҶ’    intro x hx
    55вҶ’    Hint "Use backward extraction: `rw [Finset.mem_image] at hx`
    56вҶ’    then `obtain вҹЁa, ha, hfaвҹ© := hx`."
    57вҶ’    rw [Finset.mem_image] at hx
    58вҶ’    obtain вҹЁa, ha, hfaвҹ© := hx
    59вҶ’    Hint "You have `ha : a вҲҲ Finset.range 10` and
    60вҶ’    `hfa : a + 3 = x`. Rewrite `ha` to get the bound:
    61вҶ’    `rw [Finset.mem_range] at ha` gives `a < 10`.
    62вҶ’    Then `rw [Finset.mem_range]` on the goal gives `x < 13`.
    63вҶ’    Finally `omega` closes it."
    64вҶ’    Hint (hidden := true) "Try:
    65вҶ’    `rw [Finset.mem_range] at ha`
    66вҶ’    `rw [Finset.mem_range]`
    67вҶ’    `omega`"
    68вҶ’    rw [Finset.mem_range] at ha
    69вҶ’    rw [Finset.mem_range]
    70вҶ’    omega
    71вҶ’  constructor
    72вҶ’  -- Part 3: card upper bound
    73вҶ’  В· Hint "The image can only shrink: `Finset.card_image_le` says
    74вҶ’    $|f(S)| \\leq |S|$ for any function. Apply it directly."
    75вҶ’    Hint (hidden := true) "Try `exact Finset.card_image_le`."
    76вҶ’    exact Finset.card_image_le
    77вҶ’  -- Part 4: card preserved
    78вҶ’  В· Hint "Now prove the exact count. Apply
    79вҶ’    `Finset.card_image_of_injective` вҖ” this leaves the
    80вҶ’    injectivity goal."
    81вҶ’    apply Finset.card_image_of_injective
    82вҶ’    Hint "Prove `Function.Injective (fun n => n + 3)`.
    83вҶ’    Use `show вҲҖ a b : в„•, a + 3 = b + 3 вҶ’ a = b` to restate
    84вҶ’    with the function applied, then `intro a b h; omega`."
    85вҶ’    show вҲҖ a b : в„•, a + 3 = b + 3 вҶ’ a = b
    86вҶ’    intro a b h
    87вҶ’    omega
    88вҶ’
    89вҶ’Conclusion "
    90вҶ’Congratulations вҖ” you've completed **Finset Image**!
    91вҶ’
    92вҶ’You mastered four core move families:
    93вҶ’
    94вҶ’**1. Forward membership** (witness construction):
    95вҶ’- `rw [Finset.mem_image]; use x; constructor`
    96вҶ’- Choose `x` by solving `f(x) = y`
    97вҶ’
    98вҶ’**2. Backward extraction** (witness destruction):
    99вҶ’- `rw [Finset.mem_image] at h; obtain вҹЁx, hx, hfxвҹ© := h`
   100вҶ’- Used for non-membership proofs, subset proofs, and re-use
   101вҶ’
   102вҶ’**3. Cardinality bounds**:
   103вҶ’- `Finset.card_image_le`: $|f(S)| \\leq |S|$ (always)
   104вҶ’- `Finset.card_image_of_injective`: $|f(S)| = |S|$ (when $f$ is injective)
   105вҶ’- `Finset.card_image_iff`: $|f(S)| = |S| \\iff f$ is injective on $S$
   106вҶ’- Injectivity proof: `intro a b h; omega`
   107вҶ’
   108вҶ’**4. Image algebra**:
   109вҶ’- Image distributes over union: $f(S \\cup T) = f(S) \\cup f(T)$
   110вҶ’- Image of intersection: $f(S \\cap T) \\subseteq f(S) \\cap f(T)$
   111вҶ’  (equality under injectivity)
   112вҶ’- Image of singleton: $f(\\{a\\}) = \\{f(a)\\}$
   113вҶ’
   114вҶ’| Theorem | What it says |
   115вҶ’|---|---|
   116вҶ’| `Finset.mem_image` | `y вҲҲ s.image f вҶ” вҲғ x вҲҲ s, f x = y` |
   117вҶ’| `Finset.card_image_le` | `(s.image f).card вүӨ s.card` |
   118вҶ’| `Finset.card_image_of_injective` | injective `f` implies `(s.image f).card = s.card` |
   119вҶ’| `Finset.card_image_iff` | `(s.image f).card = s.card вҶ” Set.InjOn f вҶ‘s` |
   120вҶ’
   121вҶ’**What's next**: Problem Set Finset will test these skills on fresh
   122вҶ’problems without scaffolding.
   123вҶ’"
   124вҶ’

import Game.Metadata

World "FinsetImage"
Level 21

Title "Boss: Image Membership, Bounds, and Injectivity"

Introduction "
# Boss: Putting It All Together

Consider $f(n) = n + 3$ applied to $S = \\{0, 1, \\ldots, 9\\}$.
This level integrates three core move families from the world.

**Part 1** вҖ” Forward membership (Levels 1-2):
Prove $12 \\in f(S)$ by providing the witness $9$.

**Part 2** вҖ” Subset bound via backward extraction (Levels 3-8):
Prove $f(S) \\subseteq \\{0, \\ldots, 12\\}$. Every element
$n + 3$ where $n < 10$ satisfies $n + 3 < 13$.

**Part 3** вҖ” Cardinality upper bound (Level 12):
The image can only shrink: $|f(S)| \\leq |S|$.

**Part 4** вҖ” Exact cardinality via injectivity (Levels 13-14):
Prove $|f(S)| = |S|$ by proving that $n \\mapsto n+3$ is injective.

No new moves вҖ” only moves seeded in Levels 1-19.
"

/-- Forward membership, subset bound, cardinality bounds, and injectivity. -/
Statement :
    (12 : в„•) вҲҲ (Finset.range 10).image (fun n => n + 3) вҲ§
    (Finset.range 10).image (fun n => n + 3) вҠҶ Finset.range 13 вҲ§
    ((Finset.range 10).image (fun n => n + 3)).card вүӨ
      (Finset.range 10).card вҲ§
    ((Finset.range 10).image (fun n => n + 3)).card =
      (Finset.range 10).card := by
  Hint "Use `constructor` to split into Part 1 and the remaining parts."
  constructor
  -- Part 1: 12 вҲҲ image (В· + 3) (range 10)
  В· Hint "Forward membership: `rw [Finset.mem_image]`, provide the witness.
    Solve: $n + 3 = 12$ gives $n = 9$."
    rw [Finset.mem_image]
    Hint (hidden := true) "Try `use 9`."
    use 9
    Hint "Lean verified `9 + 3 = 12`. Remaining: `9 вҲҲ Finset.range 10`.
    Use `rw [Finset.mem_range]; omega`."
    rw [Finset.mem_range]
    omega
  constructor
  -- Part 2: image (В· + 3) (range 10) вҠҶ range 13
  В· Hint "Subset goal: introduce an element with `intro x hx`,
    then extract the witness from `hx` and show `x < 13`."
    intro x hx
    Hint "Use backward extraction: `rw [Finset.mem_image] at hx`
    then `obtain вҹЁa, ha, hfaвҹ© := hx`."
    rw [Finset.mem_image] at hx
    obtain вҹЁa, ha, hfaвҹ© := hx
    Hint "You have `ha : a вҲҲ Finset.range 10` and
    `hfa : a + 3 = x`. Rewrite `ha` to get the bound:
    `rw [Finset.mem_range] at ha` gives `a < 10`.
    Then `rw [Finset.mem_range]` on the goal gives `x < 13`.
    Finally `omega` closes it."
    Hint (hidden := true) "Try:
    `rw [Finset.mem_range] at ha`
    `rw [Finset.mem_range]`
    `omega`"
    rw [Finset.mem_range] at ha
    rw [Finset.mem_range]
    omega
  constructor
  -- Part 3: card upper bound
  В· Hint "The image can only shrink: `Finset.card_image_le` says
    $|f(S)| \\leq |S|$ for any function. Apply it directly."
    Hint (hidden := true) "Try `exact Finset.card_image_le`."
    exact Finset.card_image_le
  -- Part 4: card preserved
  В· Hint "Now prove the exact count. Apply
    `Finset.card_image_of_injective` вҖ” this leaves the
    injectivity goal."
    apply Finset.card_image_of_injective
    Hint "Prove `Function.Injective (fun n => n + 3)`.
    Use `show вҲҖ a b : в„•, a + 3 = b + 3 вҶ’ a = b` to restate
    with the function applied, then `intro a b h; omega`."
    show вҲҖ a b : в„•, a + 3 = b + 3 вҶ’ a = b
    intro a b h
    omega

Conclusion "
Congratulations вҖ” you've completed **Finset Image**!

You mastered four core move families:

**1. Forward membership** (witness construction):
- `rw [Finset.mem_image]; use x; constructor`
- Choose `x` by solving `f(x) = y`

**2. Backward extraction** (witness destruction):
- `rw [Finset.mem_image] at h; obtain вҹЁx, hx, hfxвҹ© := h`
- Used for non-membership proofs, subset proofs, and re-use

**3. Cardinality bounds**:
- `Finset.card_image_le`: $|f(S)| \\leq |S|$ (always)
- `Finset.card_image_of_injective`: $|f(S)| = |S|$ (when $f$ is injective)
- `Finset.card_image_iff`: $|f(S)| = |S| \\iff f$ is injective on $S$
- Injectivity proof: `intro a b h; omega`

**4. Image algebra**:
- Image distributes over union: $f(S \\cup T) = f(S) \\cup f(T)$
- Image of intersection: $f(S \\cap T) \\subseteq f(S) \\cap f(T)$
  (equality under injectivity)
- Image of singleton: $f(\\{a\\}) = \\{f(a)\\}$

| Theorem | What it says |
|---|---|
| `Finset.mem_image` | `y вҲҲ s.image f вҶ” вҲғ x вҲҲ s, f x = y` |
| `Finset.card_image_le` | `(s.image f).card вүӨ s.card` |
| `Finset.card_image_of_injective` | injective `f` implies `(s.image f).card = s.card` |
| `Finset.card_image_iff` | `(s.image f).card = s.card вҶ” Set.InjOn f вҶ‘s` |

**What's next**: Problem Set Finset will test these skills on fresh
problems without scaffolding.
"
